title: From Divorce to Six-Figure Success: How One Mom Rewrote Her Financial Story
guestBio: Tiana Reeves is a PMP-certified project manager, Six Sigma Black Belt, and creator of the Corporate Mom Movement. She transformed her life from pregnancy loss, divorce, and corporate burnout into becoming a bestselling author and advocate for working mothers' financial independence.
excerpt: Tiana Reeves went from financial dependence and divorce to passing her PMP certification in six weeks and building a movement for corporate moms. Her story proves that your lowest moments can become your greatest plot twists.
metaDescription: Discover how Tiana Reeves transformed divorce and corporate burnout into financial independence and success through the Corporate Mom Movement.
keyTakeaways: Rich isn't about income—it's about joy, peace, and the memories you create with your family.
Teaching children financial literacy early using savings, giving, and spending buckets sets them up for lifelong success.
Imposter syndrome often means you haven't found your true purpose yet, not that you're inadequate.
Marriage should be treated like a corporate merger—make sure your values and goals align before committing.
Financial dependence costs more than money—it costs your peace, autonomy, and sense of self-worth.
Starting with what you enjoy naturally leads to finding your purpose and financial success.
Peace in your household is worth more than any amount of gold or material wealth.

pullQuotes.text: Marriage is a corporate merger — and divorce is a divestiture. Make sure your corporate cultures align before you merge.
Peace is worth more than gold. I would rather have peace in my household than all the money in the world.
Rich isn't about income — it's about joy, peace, and the memories you create.

timestamps.label: Before the brand — who was Tiana in her 20s?
When the 10-year plan fell apart: pregnancy loss
How divorce, layoffs, and failure build resilience
Why women are taught to whisper about money
Your kids think you're a superhero
Teaching kids financial literacy — the allowance system
The plot twist: PMP in 6 weeks during her hardest season
Marriage is a corporate merger — choosing a partner wisely
Peace is worth more than gold

content:
What if your biggest breakdown was actually setting you up for your greatest breakthrough? That's exactly what happened to Tiana Reeves, and her story will completely shift how you think about reinvention, money, and what it means to build a life on your own terms.

In this powerful conversation with Aimee, Tiana takes us behind the scenes of her transformation from financial dependence and corporate burnout to becoming a PMP-certified powerhouse and founder of the Corporate Mom Movement. Her journey proves that sometimes the plot twists that feel like endings are actually the beginning of your most authentic chapter.

When Life Doesn't Go According to Plan

Tiana's story begins like many of ours do—with a carefully crafted ten-year plan that life had other ideas about. After experiencing pregnancy loss and watching her marriage crumble, she found herself facing a reality she never anticipated. But here's what makes Tiana's approach different: instead of seeing these setbacks as failures, she recognized them as redirects toward something bigger.

The pivotal moment came when she made a decision that would change everything. While navigating anxiety, depression, and divorce proceedings, she decided to pursue her Project Management Professional certification. In just six weeks. During what she describes as the hardest season of her life. That's not just determination—that's a woman who understands that sometimes you have to bet on yourself when no one else will.

"I passed my PMP in six weeks during my hardest season," Tiana reflects. "That's when I realized that our lowest moments can become our greatest plot twists."

Redefining What Rich Really Means

One of the most profound shifts Tiana shares is her redefinition of wealth. After years of equating financial success with income alone, she discovered something revolutionary: rich isn't about your paycheck—it's about joy, peace, and the memories you create.

This isn't just feel-good philosophy. Tiana backs this up with practical action, particularly in how she's teaching her seven and nine-year-old children about money using Dave Ramsey's method of savings, giving, and spending buckets. She's not just building her own financial literacy—she's ensuring the next generation starts with a healthier relationship with money than many of us had.

The reality is that most of us form our money stories by age eight, often inheriting limiting beliefs about what we deserve or what's possible. Tiana is intentionally breaking that cycle, showing her children that money is a tool for creating the life you want, not a source of anxiety or control.

Marriage as a Corporate Merger

Perhaps one of Tiana's most striking insights is her reframe of marriage and partnership. "Marriage is a corporate merger," she explains, "and divorce is a divestiture. You need to make sure your corporate cultures align before you merge."

This isn't about removing romance from relationships—it's about being strategic and intentional about one of the most important decisions you'll make in your life. When you're choosing a partner as a grown woman, especially as a mother, you're essentially interviewing someone for a role in your life's company. Their values, goals, and vision need to complement yours, not compete with them.

The cost of financial dependence, as Tiana learned firsthand, extends far beyond money. It costs you your peace, your autonomy, and often your sense of self-worth. Her journey to financial independence wasn't just about building wealth—it was about reclaiming her power to make choices from a place of strength rather than necessity.

The Corporate Mom Movement

What emerged from Tiana's transformation is something much bigger than personal success—it's a movement. The Corporate Mom Movement isn't just about helping working mothers advance in their careers; it's about changing the narrative around ambitious motherhood entirely.

Tiana recognized that ambitious mothers make people uncomfortable because we challenge traditional expectations. We're not content to choose between being good mothers and successful professionals. We're rewriting the rules and showing that excellence in both areas isn't just possible—it's powerful.

Through her work, her upcoming children's book "Shhh, My Mommy's a Superhero," and her memoir "Living in the Black," Tiana is giving other women permission to embrace their full potential without apology.

From Imposter Syndrome to Purpose

One of Tiana's most liberating insights addresses something we all struggle with: imposter syndrome. Instead of viewing it as evidence that we don't belong, she reframes it as a sign that we haven't found our true purpose yet. "Imposter syndrome doesn't mean you're a fraud—it means you haven't found your purpose yet."

This shift changes everything. Instead of shrinking back when you feel out of place, you can lean into the discomfort as information. It's telling you that you're meant for something different, something bigger, something more aligned with who you're becoming.

Peace Over Everything

Throughout this conversation, one theme emerges clearly: the value of peace. "Peace is worth more than gold," Tiana declares. "I would rather have peace in my household than all the money in the world."

This isn't about settling for less—it's about understanding that true wealth includes emotional and mental well-being. It's about creating a life where success doesn't come at the cost of your sanity or your children's sense of security.

Tiana's story is proof that you can rebuild your life from the ground up and create something even better than what you originally planned. Her journey from pregnancy loss, divorce, and corporate burnout to PMP-certified powerhouse and movement leader shows us that our setbacks don't define our ceiling—our response to them does.

Whether you're navigating your own plot twist or simply ready to step more fully into your power, Tiana's story offers both inspiration and practical wisdom. Sometimes the life you're meant to live is on the other side of the life that fell apart. And sometimes, that falling apart is exactly what needed to happen to set you free.

quiz.title: What's Your Wealth Mindset?
quiz.description: Discover how you really think about money, success, and what it means to build a life on your own terms. Your mindset might surprise you!

questions.question: When you think about your ideal financial future, what matters most to you?

options.text: Having a solid emergency fund and knowing I can handle whatever life throws at me
Never having to ask permission or depend on someone else for financial decisions
Earning money through work that feels meaningful and aligned with my values
Being able to create amazing memories and experiences with the people I love

questions.question: You're facing a major life transition. What's your first instinct?

options.text: Create a detailed plan and build up my safety net before making any big moves
See this as my chance to break free from anything that's been holding me back
Use this opportunity to realign with what truly matters to me
Focus on maintaining peace and happiness for myself and my family during the change

questions.question: How do you define being 'successful' in life?

options.text: Having enough resources to feel confident and prepared for the future
Being completely self-sufficient and able to make my own choices
Making a positive impact while also achieving financial stability
Living with joy, peace, and strong relationships with the people I care about

questions.question: When you're stressed about money, what bothers you most?

options.text: The uncertainty and not knowing if I'll have enough for unexpected expenses
Feeling like I have to compromise my independence or ask others for help
Worrying that I might have to take work that doesn't align with my values
The impact financial stress has on my peace of mind and family relationships

questions.question: You have an unexpected windfall. What's your first move?

options.text: Put most of it into savings and investments to build long-term security
Use it to eliminate any debts or dependencies that limit my freedom
Invest in education or business opportunities that align with my goals
Create some amazing experiences while also being responsible with the rest

questions.question: What motivates you most when it comes to your career?

options.text: Building expertise and a reputation that provides steady, reliable income
Having control over my schedule, decisions, and financial independence
Doing work that feels meaningful and contributes to something bigger
Maintaining a balance that allows me to prioritize what brings me joy

results.title: The Security Builder
results.description: You value stability and creating a solid foundation for yourself and your loved ones. Financial peace means having enough saved for emergencies and knowing you can weather life's storms. You understand that true wealth includes emotional security, not just numbers in a bank account. This episode will resonate with your journey of building sustainable success while redefining what 'enough' really means.

results.title: The Freedom Seeker
results.description: You're driven by the desire for independence and the ability to make choices without financial constraints holding you back. Money represents options and autonomy to you. You've likely experienced or fear the trap of financial dependence that limits your ability to live authentically. Tiana's story of breaking free and creating her own path will inspire your next bold move toward true freedom.

results.title: The Purpose Pioneer
results.description: You believe that meaningful work and financial success can absolutely go hand in hand. You're not just building wealth—you're building a legacy that aligns with your values. You understand that when you start with what brings you joy, the money often follows naturally. This episode will validate your approach and show you how to turn your passion into sustainable six-figure success.

results.title: The Joy Maximizer
results.description: You've discovered the secret that many people miss: being rich isn't about your income—it's about the peace, joy, and memories you create with the people you love. You measure wealth in experiences and relationships, not just dollars. You'll love hearing how Tiana redefined success and created a life where financial stability serves her happiness, not the other way around.

transcript: 🎶 Music Outro 🎶 Today on the Manage Her podcast, we're talking about reinvention, resilience, and what it truly means to build the life on your own terms. My guest is a banker turned brand builder, author, podcast host, and creator of the Corporate Mom Movement, a woman who understands firsthand what it takes to navigate career pivots, single motherhood, financial leadership, and personal transformation. After experiencing major life shifts, she didn't shrink. She expanded. She stepped into visibility. built her platform, and began helping women think differently about wealth, work, and possibility. With a forthcoming children's book, a powerful memoir titled Living in the Black, and a growing voice in conversations around money and identity, she is helping shape how the next generation understands leadership at home and beyond. Please welcome Tiana Reeves to the Oh, thank you so much. I'm honored to be here. I'm going to tell you, that intro made me feel exhausted. I'm like, I do all of that? How? It's one, you know, it's one minute at a time as a mom, isn't it? It's like, I feel like we all hear our accomplishments and think, I didn't do all that. But you did. Yeah. Crazy to hear your resume just go. Yeah. It's amazing what you can do when you're motivated. I feel like kids light a fire under our feet that we really didn't have as, you know, as single women or with no children. And it's, you know, we become moms and we have to step up and step in and really become. Yeah, we definitely do. We do things that we didn't think were even possible. I know. I was thinking about that this morning. I was thinking of how much the motherhood experience pushes our... as human beings, how you didn't think you could only get two hours of sleep for a month or two months or three months when your baby was a newborn. But you actually can. I know. Right? Yeah, you actually can. And I don't ever want to do it again. Yeah. No, I'm super glad I'm actually done finally. I still have a three-year-old, Tiana. Oh, my goodness. I will be praying for you every night. He's a good sleeper, though. But I'm sure he sleeps through the night. He does. He's a good thinker. Thank the Lord. He's a good sleeper. And he really always has been. But yeah. Oh, my gosh. So I need to learn all about you. So before the brand, the books and the corporate mom movement, who were you when you were just trying to figure life out? Yeah. So when I was in my 20s, I was fresh out of college and I had a 10 year plan. I was one of those people who were like, I already know what's going to happen and how it's going to happen. I'm going to get married, have fun, build a career, then have kids at the end of the 10 years, because then, you know, we'd be old at 30. And it was pregnancy that kind of derailed that. So at the time, my husband, who was at the time my boyfriend, and I had an unexpected pregnancy. And it was not a successful pregnancy. It was the first of many pregnancy losses. And it derailed my plans because where I thought I'm going to have kids whenever I want to, it was like, no, no, you're not. And so then it made me question all sorts of things like, Is there anything else in my plan that's flawed or that's going to derail things? And so it was a challenge for me to go from I had this meticulous plan to, okay, now what? Oh, yeah, totally. Learning to be resilient in the face of actual life. I think when you're a little kid, you think things are going to be very different than the actual experience of living life as a human being on the planet Earth. You know, I have a friend who wrote a book and it's called Life is Written in Pencil. And it's kind of like, you know, you kind of you do when you're a little kid, you think everything's written in Sharpie and, you know, it's going to be this permanent thing. And, you know, there will be no changes made. But as you become a grown up, you realize it's actually written in pencil and there will be an A through Z plan. Yeah. If not, one's going to get created for you. Yeah, exactly. So it's all about reinvention. And like many women experience a life event, like a divorce or a layoff or a major identity shift, and that could either break them or build them. I've been through it myself. Many women experience a life event, a divorce, a layoff, a major identity shift that could either break them or build them. I know I went through this. So what did that season demand of you and who have you become to walk through it? I went through it all. Divorce, I went through at least maybe two or three different layoffs in my career. And the biggest thing that I had to learn was that social norms don't define you. If you let that hiccup that happens in life stop you and say, oh my gosh, I didn't do this perfectly, or I didn't get picked in the cut when there was a layoff, or my marriage ended, I failed, then you'll start to get stuck in this rut again. And trying to fit that narrative will make you feel anxious and depressed. But the reality is no one is fitting that script perfectly. But when we have those moments happen, we're thinking, oh my gosh, we've just created the biggest faux pas ever. Yeah, it's I remember that too. You know, I'm divorced also. And I felt that societal pressure. I felt that I felt that internal failure, that judgment of myself instead of just experiencing what it was. and taking that as a life lesson. Okay, so I didn't choose my partner that well. Maybe I didn't communicate well enough. I don't know. There was some systemic failure in this, but what can I learn from it? How can I do it better? That's what we have to do in life. How can we adjust and conquer from our experiences and stop judging each other? Life happens. Life is messy and no one's perfect. 100%. There's a concept in Agile methodology that says, Encourage your team to fail forward. So things are going to happen, but how do you become resilient and how do you grow from that hiccup that happened? Yeah, I think my mom was like the master of this in my life. So I was very fortunate to have a mentor, my mother, who was constantly failing forward. It didn't matter what happened in her life, but it always somehow propelled her forward. Every time she failed, it moved her in the right direction. And it was neat to see the way that she would just kind of dust herself off, get back up and keep going. She never got down for very long. Yeah. You know, maybe she cried in the closet for an hour, but then she got up. That's what I do. She got up and she kept moving on, you know, and that's I mean, I think that's I mean, who hasn't gone in the bathroom and had a good cry? You know, sometimes you need a good cry and then you pick yourself up. You're not a real adult unless you do. That's right. Yeah. And you need to give yourself time to process your emotions. It's okay to feel sad. It's okay to have negative emotions and process those things. That's not something that we need to run away from either. I feel like in our society, we've been running away from that stuff too. And maybe it's time that we can own that stuff a little bit more and be okay with the fact that we do have emotions and we learn from them. Yeah, absolutely. They're crucial, right? So you created the phrase, the corporate mom. Corporate mom. What does that identity give women permission to stop apologizing for? Different, yeah, different things were on my feed and things that were going on in the world. And I started to kind of share my thoughts or opinions or insight, some of it with some like goofy parodies and then some of them with like some real heart to hearts. And social media actually made Corporate Mom the way that it is. People engaging on the posts asking, what do you think about this? Or, oh my gosh, that's so funny, I relate to that. And it just kind of took off from there. A happy accident is, what's his name? Bob, oh gosh, the guy that's the painter, Bob. Ross. Bob Ross. Happy little accidents. Yes, is a happy little accident. Isn't that life? I love that. I know some of the biggest things in life are like, just a little something it was I was having a conversation with Steve Edwards of Good Day LA and he said his name basically came from some producer guy who looked at him he was he told him his name is Bob Schwartz and he goes well what's your middle name he goes Edward he goes what do you think about Steve Edwards he goes that's fine and that's how he got his name and that's been his name for 50 years Other people made it happen, not necessarily you. Or it's just like this instantaneous decision sometimes and then you create an identity and then there it is. And you're a corporate mom, you know, there you are, corporate mom, Deanna Reeves. Yeah, right. So why do you think ambition in mothers still makes some people uncomfortable? I think it makes people feel uncomfortable because the American workplace doesn't really give space for women to choose motherhood and like have that work-life balance. Today, when companies will do things like allow a parent to volunteer at their kid's school or take time off without clocking out for vacation hours, it's seen as progressive. It's not seen as the normal thing to do. We're not there yet. And our society will say things like, oh, it's so great. Dad is here at the doctor's office, but it's shameful if mom isn't. And so when we have those different things that happen in our society, mothers aren't encouraged to be leaders in the workplace because if they are leaders, performing in the workplace, they're being shunned for not leading their home so we need to celebrate women who take on more. We need to say, you know what, that is great that you are working at home and that you're working outside your home and that you're at your kid's school. Like, oh my goodness, you're such a powerhouse, like uplifting that and not just treating it as, oh, well, you're doing what you're supposed to do, or you better take on all this. Otherwise, what kind of a woman are you? Yeah. So the balance between the work-life balance for the modern mother is something that is, it needs a little work, I think. And we as women need to cut each other a little bit of slack too, and give each other some love and respect on what a hard job it is to be a mom and to be a working mom. Being a mom alone, just being a stay-at-home mom is a hard job. But being a work mom. And you still have to do the home stuff, too. It's a lot. It's like if we could all just give each other a little bit of grace while we get through this transition. You know, I think we're making a transition as women right now. I really do. And I think we're trying to find a better balance for our families and for ourselves. But I think I do. I really feel like we're in a transitional and pivotal moment in human civilization for women right now. Absolutely. We need to normalize. It's great you fed your kids, even though it was microwave chicken nuggets. You fed them, you know. They did eat. I know. And I'm always talking to my other moms, like, how can we do this better? How can we support each other? You know, is there a way that we could create a group of working moms or even maybe some moms that are stay at home moms that we all figure out one night a week that one mom cooks for maybe four or five people? And then, you know, is there a way, could we be doing this better? Could we be cooperating more? Is there a way to lighten this load for women that we can help each other out? That's one of my big questions to women in general. I love that, Amy. Like giving women permission to celebrate you cooked at least one meal or for the moms who say, I want to do blue apron or fresh, we'll just stick with blue apron because I can't remember. Yeah, totally. All the meal preps. Yeah. That, hey, at least you thought enough to prep and you're cooking something together and maybe you're bringing your kids in the kitchen. That's good. You don't have to cook every day. And even for the moms who, work at home as, you know, a stay-at-home mom, you're busy too. So even if you don't cook every night, that doesn't mean that you're not meeting the expectations of a stay-at-home mom. Yeah, totally. We just need to give each other a little more grace. And I look forward to women coming together again. I feel like we were really pushed into these isolated little groups of, you know, the nuclear family group. And I just don't think that that is working for us. I think that we are burnt out and stressed out because we are trying to do too much all by ourselves. I think that there could be so much more joy and happiness for us and our families if we figure out how to work together, especially as working moms. I really think that we can figure out a way to do this better. And I feel like with technology, we'll figure it out. We'll have an app for it. I'm calling on a woman to figure this out. Do you think, okay, why are women still taught to whisper about money? And what changes in a woman when she finally becomes financially confident? There's two things that kind of come to mind when I think about women and money. So the first part is when you're a young woman and you're dating and you're thinking about heterosexual relationships, we are taught that men should be the provider and that you find a man who is set up to be a provider and help them to grow in it. And then you also have like the women's movement where you are able to be educated and you're able to be in the workplace. And then I'll talk a little bit about Black culture because in Black culture, there's a big emphasis on being an independent woman. Those that are in my age group, y'all know. And it's about being a woman and having your own job and having your own house and having your own money and that you were this independent woman. And it ended up shifting to being, oh, well, you're one of those independent women. You can't really be a wife or you really can't be in that family nucleus because independent women, they just don't function that way is kind of how it's been framed up. Now there's another conflict where the man's supposed to be the provider. How do you get into a heterosexual relationship with a man? if he's not being led to provide because you've built yourself up this much. So sometimes we have to shrink ourselves and not talk about those things. So that way, you know, he can feel strong and powerful and confident as a provider. But providing isn't just financially. There's other ways that he can be a provider. But I think until we have that culture shift to be comfortable in different norms, to say you can still be in your femininity and your masculinity, and this is how we'll do it for our family. I think we'll still see women just be more quieter about what they do and how they do it. Yeah, I agree. Yeah, I think culture is so important. And as we move forward through this transitional phase, I feel like communication is going to be just such a big part of how we shift culture. It's having conversations like this that help the culture shift. Actually, our viewers are equally male and female, which I think is very interesting. So it's funny that I think men are just as curious about how women are feeling in this time and this shift as we are becoming more aware of the situation that we are now in. And then my mom became, was the first working mom in our generation. And so it's, you know, it's new. It's new. Like it's relatively generationally new for us. To be in this position. And if they see their moms doing it all, they're like, that's what moms do. And so moms oftentimes stop celebrating the things that they do because they don't feel like that they did anything that's valuable or that their kids even care. Trust me, your kids care. It may be like 24, 48 hours before you get the ice cream, but mom's going to be like, OK. Or dad might go, go ask your mom. Oh, that too. Like, well, I don't know. She didn't tell me to own that. She told me to own bath time, not ice cream. I do not own ice cream. It's okay, guys. If you're listening, just own the ice cream and just say, I made an executive decision. I'm so sorry we did not convene on a meeting before I made this decision. I like that. Yeah, I think it's okay. It's okay for you guys to own some stuff. Just be like, I decided to own this for us because... I thought this would be helpful for you. Absolutely. It's just ice cream. We'll be fine. It's just ice cream. It will be okay. Totally. I agree with you. I think growing up with my mom, my mom was such a superhero. Honestly, she was entrepreneur of the year in 1988. And I used to get up at 5.30 in the morning just to watch her put her makeup on before she went to work. I just was so in awe of her. She was so beautiful and powerful. And I really looked up to her so much. And she was such a tremendous role model for work ethic, almost to the point of being over a workaholic, which I did learn from my mother.
